query: effectiveness of MVA85A vaccine against smallpox

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of small pox. Review the presentation of a patient with small pox. Summarize the treatment of small pox. Outline the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team members to be aware of the clinical presentation of smallpox and report it efficiently to prevent an outbreak of this disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccination has been successful in the eradication of smallpox globally. There have been several smallpox vaccines developed over time, beginning with variolation, the deliberate inoculation of infectious smallpox from the pustule of an infectious person to a healthy, nonimmune contact to induce a more mild disease course. Descriptions of variolation are found from as early as 1500 BC. This practice carried significant risk including severe disease and mortality as well as the potential for community spread of infection. In 1798, Edward Jenner’s publication of his research confirming that cowpox protected against smallpox infection led to the adoption of cowpox vaccination. By 1900, cowpox was no longer the virus used for vaccination, but vaccinia virus, which is more closely related to horsepox. Vaccinia became the virus used for large-scale global vaccination programs during the 20th century, but potential risks persisted. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Treatment / Management -- 5. Vaccinations
retrieved_docs.content: Recommendations for vaccination is based on the antibody deficiency severity [54] : Routine schedule for inactivated or subunit vaccines in mild deficiency: DTaP (diphtheria, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis vaccine) HAV (Hepatitis A virus) HBV (hepatitis B virus) HIB (Haemophilus influenzae type B) HPV (human papillomavirus) Influenza Meningococcal Pneumococcal Polio (intramuscular) Anthrax JE (Japanese encephalitis) Typhoid (intramuscular). Rabies Routine schedule for inactivated or subunit vaccines in severe deficiency: HPV Influenza Anthrax Rabies Recommended administration of live-attenuated vaccines in mild deficiency (likely benefit but possible harm): MMR (measles-mumps-rubella) Rotavirus Varicella Herpes Zoster Smallpox (for pre-exposure) BCG (Bacille Calmette-Guerin) Live-attenuated vaccines are not recommended in severe deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The replication-defective modified vaccinia Ankara vaccine is a two-shot series, four weeks apart, with a superior safety profile compared to first and second-generation smallpox vaccines. Unlike live vaccinia virus preparations, administering modified vaccinia, Ankara does not create a skin lesion or pose a risk of local or disseminated spread. [25] In addition, clinical trials have shown that modified vaccinia Ankara is safe and stimulates antibody production in patients with atopy and compromised immune systems, which are known contraindications to live vaccinia administration. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Live attenuated vaccines: Induce both humoral and cellular responses; contraindicated in pregnancy and immunocompromised states. Examples include adenovirus, polio (Sabin), varicella, smallpox, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG), yellow fever, influenza (intranasal), MMR, and rotavirus. Killed or inactivated vaccines: Induce only humoral response; examples include rabies, influenza (injection), polio (Salk), and hepatitis A. Subunit vaccines: Examples include hepatitis B virus, human papillomavirus (types 6, 11, 16, and 18), acellular pertussis, Neisseria meningitidis , Streptococcus pneumoniae , and Haemophilus influenzae  type b . Toxoid vaccine: Examples include Clostridium tetani and Corynebacterium diphtheriae.

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Clinical Significance -- Smallpox
retrieved_docs.content: The overall mortality of smallpox is approximately 30% in unvaccinated individuals. Smallpox vaccination within 72 hours of exposure can prevent the disease; vaccination within 3 to 7 days will not prevent the disease but will limit symptoms. [33] Cidofovir is theorized to be a treatment for short-term prophylaxis against smallpox. Consult the CDC for treatment recommendations.

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Post-eradication, vaccine researchers have utilized improved technology to develop tissue-culture-based live vaccines, live attenuated virus vaccines, and viral subunit vaccine products.  These are necessary to improve vaccine safety within the global context of an eradicated infection. Current smallpox vaccine recommendations include only personnel at increased special risk of exposure such as researchers, some healthcare workers, and some United States military personnel. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Smallpox is the first human infectious disease to be successfully eradicated worldwide. It remains of clinical concern because of the potential for release and weaponization. Smallpox is a member of the viral family poxvirus, genus orthopoxvirus, and species variola virus. This disease presents with a non-specific febrile prodrome of high fever, chills, vomiting, abdominal pain, headache, and backache. The skin lesions occur 1-3 days later and first begin on the forearms or face and spread to the rest of the body. This activity illustrates the evaluation and treatment of smallpox and describes the role of the interprofessional team in managing those with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccine-associated adverse events from live vaccinia virus can be serious and include generalized vaccinia, eczema vaccinatum, progressive vaccinia, and postvaccinial encephalitis. Vaccine adverse events can also cause symptoms in others through contact with vaccine site and unintended inoculation, or from vaccinated mother to fetus. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For individuals exposed to the virus, temperature and symptoms should be monitored twice daily for 21 days because that is the accepted upper limit of the mpox incubation period. Infectiousness aligns with symptom onset; therefore, close contacts need not isolate while asymptomatic. In some cases, post-exposure vaccination with modified vaccinia, Ankara vaccine (smallpox and mpox vaccine, live, non-replicating) is recommended. Contact between broken skin or mucous membranes and an infected patient’s body fluids, respiratory droplets, or scabs is considered a “high risk” exposure and warrants post-exposure vaccination as soon as possible. According to the CDC, vaccination within four days of exposure may prevent disease onset, and vaccination within 14 days may reduce disease severity.

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The spread of infectious diseases requires a susceptible population and opportunities for transmission. Individual and herd immunity to mpox, previously achieved through widespread vaccinia vaccination, has declined since the 1980s, increasing human susceptibility to outbreaks. [3] [Level 3] In addition, interim sociopolitical and ecological changes in endemic regions likely increased human exposure to animal reservoirs. [16] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mpox (Monkeypox) is a zoonotic orthopoxvirus that incidentally causes disease in humans similar to smallpox, although with notably lower mortality. This virus is clinically relevant because it is endemic to western and central Africa, with outbreaks in the Western Hemisphere related to the exotic pet trade and international travel. Coincident immunity to the mpox virus was previously achieved with vaccinia vaccination; however, eradicating smallpox and subsequent lack of vaccination efforts paved the way for monkeypox to gain clinical relevance. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of monkeypox infections in humans and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition and minimizing disease outbreaks.

retrieved_docs.title: Typhoid Vaccine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Endemic countries
retrieved_docs.content: The WHO gives the highest priority for TCV use to highly endemic countries and those with high rates of XDR typhoid. [4] TCVs are preferred by the WHO to prevent typhoid fever due to their immunological properties, efficacy in young children, and prolonged duration of immunity compared to unconjugated vaccines. [4] Previously, only Typbar-TCV ® was WHO-prequalified, raising supply concerns. Since then, Vi-CMR 197 TM and SKYTyphoid TM have become prequalified. A stable and cost-effective vaccine supply requires multiple vaccine types and manufacturers; several newer vaccines are at various stages of development. [14] The Vi-rEPA vaccine is not currently commercially available.

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Remaining variola virus stock has been restricted to the WHO Collaborating Centers on Smallpox and other Poxvirus Infections at the CDC and the Russian State Research Centre of Virology and Biotechnology (SRC VB VECTOR) labs. After the events of Sept. 11, 20001 in the United States, the concern arose for the deliberate release of the virus as an act of bioterrorism, and research for medical countermeasures including vaccines and anti-viral medications was renewed. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a kombinované vakcíny (combined vaccine), indicating that it incorporates multiple antigens into a single immunization preparation. This categorization highlights the vaccine’s role in delivering protection against several diseases—such as diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and polio—through one combined formulation, which can simplify vaccination schedules and improve patient compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 409
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccines (vakcíny) represent a broad class of immunizations that include several specialized subcategories. Combined bacterial and viral vaccines, referred to as “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined,” are a distinct group within the overall vaccine category, and the DTPP vaccine is an example that belongs to this combined bacterial‑viral group. Toxoidy (toxoid vaccines) is a child subcategory of vaccines, focusing on inactivated toxins as immunogens. Viral vaccines constitute another subclass of vaccines; the active ingredients of these formulations are viral antigens (antigeny virové). The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to the general vaccine class, indicating that it is also a vaccine. Together, these relationships illustrate how various vaccine types—combined bacterial/viral formulations, toxoid preparations, and viral antigen‑based products—are organized under the overarching concept of vaccines, highlighting their shared purpose of preventing infectious diseases and clarifying how specific products such as DTPP and SNC‑rCTB fit within this taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” (vaccines) is positioned within a broader taxonomy that includes both “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” and the more general category “medications and vaccines,” indicating that vaccines are considered a subset of combined bacterial‑viral immunizations as well as of pharmaceutical products overall. Within the vaccine hierarchy, “toxoidy” is identified as a child category of “vakcíny,” representing toxoid‑based vaccines as a specific type of vaccine. Individual immunizations are linked to these categories: the “SNC‑rCTB vaccine” is mapped to the overarching “vakcíny” class, and the “DTPP vaccine” is recognized as a member of the “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” group. This structured relationship clarifies how vaccines are organized into broader medication groups, how sub‑types such as toxoid vaccines fit within the overall vaccine category, and how specific vaccines are classified within combined bacterial‑viral immunization sets, supporting coherent medical classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 919
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatologické látky are classified within the broader category of medications and vaccines and include keratolytika as a specific subclass. The term vakcíny, which denotes vaccines, is considered a subcategory of both bacterial and viral vaccines, combined and the general group medications and vaccines. Specific vaccines such as typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine, S typhi Ty2 strain 0.05 MG/ML Injectable Solution [Typhim VI] and the SNC‑rCTB vaccine are members of the vakcíny group. The typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine contains fenol as an inactive ingredient. Additionally, the DTPP vaccine is identified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines category. This network of relationships highlights how dermatological agents, various vaccine formulations, and their components are organized within the overarching framework of medications and vaccines, aiding clinicians in understanding classification, composition, and grouping of therapeutic products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” is classified under the broader category of viral vaccines, indicating that viral vaccines serve as the parent classification for these vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is classified under the category vakcíny, and vakcíny is identified as a subcategory of viral vaccines; therefore, the SNC‑rCTB vaccine belongs to the broader group of viral vaccines, reflecting its use of viral components to stimulate an immune response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial and viral vaccines, combined are classified as a type of vakcíny, indicating that they belong to the broader vaccine category. The vakcíny group itself is positioned under viral vaccines, making viral vaccines the parent class of vakcíny. The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to the vakcíny category, confirming its inclusion among vaccines. Additionally, the DTPP vaccine is identified as a member of the bacterial and viral vaccines, combined group, linking it directly to this specific combined vaccine subclass. Together, these relationships illustrate how combined bacterial‑viral vaccines fit within the hierarchical structure of vaccine classifications and how individual vaccines such as SNC‑rCTB and DTPP are situated within that framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” group, indicating that it contains antigens from both bacterial and viral pathogens and is therefore a combined vaccine. In this ontology, the category “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” is regarded as a specific type of vakcíny (vaccines), placing combined bacterial‑viral formulations within the broader vaccine family. Additionally, the classification system links vakcíny to a parent category of viral vaccines, suggesting that vaccines are organized under the larger umbrella of viral vaccine concepts while still encompassing formulations that also target bacterial agents. The term kombinované vakcíny (“combined vaccines”) is used to map the DTPP vaccine, reinforcing its identity as a vaccine that integrates multiple pathogen components. Together, these relationships illustrate how the DTPP vaccine fits into a hierarchical structure that connects combined bacterial‑viral vaccines to the general vaccine category and situates that category within the overarching group of viral vaccines.

retrieved_docs.content: shampoo (9 mg/mL). * **Antiparasitic/dermatologic shampoos** – lindane (10 mg/mL) and clobetasol propionate shampoo (0.5 mg/mL). * **Vaccines** – Ad26 vector COVID‑19 vaccine (100 000 000 UNT/mL) and protein subunit vaccine NVX‑CoV2373 (0.01 mg/mL). The combined emphasis on CCB pharmacology and the universal role of hydrochloric acid as an excipient underscores key considerations in drug formulation, mechanism‑based therapy, and patient‑centered care across cardiovascular, neurological, infectious, hormonal, and vaccine domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 156
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial and viral vaccines, combined are recognized as a specific category within the broader group of vaccines (vakcíny). Within the vaccine hierarchy, toxoidy is identified as a child subcategory of vakcíny, indicating that toxoid‑based preparations are a distinct type of vaccine. Although vakcíny are positioned under the larger umbrella of viral vaccines, the classification also accommodates combined bacterial‑viral formulations, reflecting the diversity of vaccine compositions. The typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine, S typhi Ty2 strain 0.05 MG/ML Injectable Solution [Typhim VI] is classified as a vakcíny and contains fenol as an inactive ingredient, illustrating the role of excipients in vaccine formulations. The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to the vakcíny category, further exemplifying the inclusion of various immunogenic products within the vaccine taxonomy. DTPP vaccine is described as a combined vaccine (kombinované vakcíny) and is a member of the bacterial and viral vaccines, combined group, highlighting its composition of both bacterial and viral antigens. Finally, viral antigens (antigeny virové) are identified as active ingredients of viral vaccines, underscoring the fundamental immunogenic component that drives protective immunity in these products. Together, these relationships delineate a structured view of vaccine types, their subcategories, constituent ingredients, and the hierarchical connections among bacterial, viral, and combined immunizations.
